skill.name: python-executor
skill.description: Runs Python code through the inference.sh CLI in a sandboxed Python 3.10 environment with common data, web, media, document, and 3D libraries.
skill.summary: Run Python scripts in an inference.sh sandbox with common data, scraping, media, and document libraries.
skill.author: inferen-sh

security_audit.summary: Static command findings are mostly false positives from Markdown fences, inline package names, and documented CLI examples. The skill still has medium risk because it intentionally allows user-supplied Python execution through the infsh CLI, includes network-capable libraries, and returns files from a sandboxed run. No evidence found of malicious intent, prompt injection, credential theft, or hidden exfiltration in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: Intentional Remote Python Code Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill grants Bash access to infsh commands and documents inputs that accept arbitrary Python code. This is expected for the skill, but it can execute unsafe user-provided logic in the external sandbox if misused.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool and input schema directly show that Python code is passed to the infsh executor. The behavior is intentional and documented, so the risk is capability-based rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Network-Capable Execution Environment
medium_findings.description: The documented runtime includes HTTP clients, browser automation, web scraping libraries, and API call examples. User scripts can make outbound requests from the sandbox, which may expose supplied data to third-party services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network libraries and request examples are explicit in the documentation. No malicious endpoint is present, but outbound network capability is clearly supported.

low_findings.title: Returned Output Files Require Data Care
low_findings.description: Files written to the outputs directory are automatically returned by the execution service. This is useful, but users should avoid writing secrets or private data to returned files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file return behavior is clearly documented. The exposure risk depends on user code and input data rather than hidden skill behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Shell Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick detections map to Markdown code fences, inline package names, and example infsh commands. These are not hidden shell execution paths inside the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows Markdown examples and package formatting, not executable source code embedded in the skill. The real execution path is the declared infsh tool capability.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The high static finding on line 3 appears to match text in the description and inference.sh URL. No cryptographic operation or weak hash algorithm is present in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is descriptive metadata with a service URL and library names. I did not find evidence of cryptographic code or a weak algorithm call.

low_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance Finding Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The static network reconnaissance hit maps to an important note that says execution runs in an isolated subprocess. It is a safety claim, not scanning or discovery behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line contains documentation about safe execution. I did not find evidence of port scanning, host discovery, or network reconnaissance commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Arbitrary Python Code Submitted to External Executor
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs agents to submit Python code through infsh app run. This pattern should be shown with a marketplace warning because it executes user-supplied logic outside the local model context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code input field and CLI invocation are explicit. The risk is inherent to remote code execution tools even when sandboxed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound HTTP and Browser Automation Support
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented libraries include HTTP clients, web scraping frameworks, and browser automation tools. These capabilities can contact external sites when user code requests it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The supported network libraries and API example are directly documented. There is no hidden destination, so this is a capability warning.

content.user_title: Run Python Tasks in a Remote Sandbox
content.value_statement: Agents often need Python for analysis, scraping, file generation, and media processing. This skill routes those jobs to an inference.sh sandbox with common libraries already installed.
actual_capabilities: Runs Python 3.10 code through the inference.sh CLI.
Uses installed libraries for pandas, NumPy, Matplotlib, requests, BeautifulSoup, Playwright, Pillow, OpenCV, MoviePy, and trimesh.
Creates returned files from the outputs directory, including charts, CSV files, videos, PDFs, and 3D models.
Supports default and high-memory runtime variants.
Runs non-interactive scripts with configurable timeout and output capture.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and an authenticated infsh session.
Runs in a CPU-only environment without GPU or dedicated ML libraries.
Does not support interactive prompts, windows, or blocking display calls.
Network requests and file outputs depend on the remote sandbox policy.

use_cases.title: Analyze a Dataset
use_cases.description: Process tabular data, create summaries, and return charts or CSV outputs from a sandboxed Python run.

use_cases.title: Extract Web Data
use_cases.description: Fetch pages, parse HTML, and transform public web data into structured outputs for later review.

use_cases.title: Generate Media Files
use_cases.description: Create images, short videos, PDFs, or 3D assets with common Python media libraries.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Simple Python Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to run a short Python script that prints the installed pandas version and explains the result.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Chart File
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to analyze this small dataset and return a bar chart saved in the outputs directory.

prompt_templates.title: Scrape and Summarize a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to fetch the target page, extract the title and headings, and summarize the visible structure.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multi-Step Asset Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to generate a CSV summary, a visualization, and a PDF report from the provided source data.

output_examples.input: Analyze two columns of sales data and create a chart.
output_examples.output: Returns a short analysis summary and a chart file from the outputs directory.

output_examples.input: Fetch a public profile page and extract basic metadata.
output_examples.output: Returns the page title, selected fields, and a concise note about missing data.

output_examples.input: Create a simple 3D sphere model for download.
output_examples.output: Returns a model file and a summary of the generated mesh size.

best_practices: Keep scripts focused and set a timeout that matches the workload.
Save generated artifacts under outputs so they are returned reliably.
Review any network target and avoid sending secrets to external services.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for untrusted code without reviewing what it will run.
Do not write credentials, tokens, or private records into returned output files.
Do not use interactive display calls when saved files are required.

faq.question: What does this skill execute?
faq.answer: It executes Python code through the inference.sh python-executor app using the infsh CLI.

faq.question: Does it run locally?
faq.answer: No. It submits work to an inference.sh sandbox through the local CLI.

faq.question: Can it access the network?
faq.answer: The documented environment includes HTTP clients and scraping tools, so user scripts may make network requests.

faq.question: How are files returned?
faq.answer: Files saved under outputs are detected and returned by the execution service.

faq.question: Does it support GPU workloads?
faq.answer: No. The documented environment is CPU-only and points users to dedicated AI apps for ML workloads.

faq.question: What should users avoid?
faq.answer: Users should avoid running unknown code, sending secrets to network services, or writing private data to outputs.
